meta.title: Should the university make in-person attendance optional given AI tutoring?
meta.subtitle: Decision Brief — possibility map and recommendation

slides.title: Should attendance go optional now that AI tutoring beats lecture?
slides.subtitle: Decision Brief — possibility map and recommendation

slides.title: Universal optional attendance is strictly dominated; ship a 3-year department pilot with mandatory first-year and dual-track compliance
slides.action: Do NOT make attendance universally optional. Run a 3-year opt-in pilot in 1-2 departments + mandatory first-year residential + dual-track F-1/Title IV compliance with required in-person seminars + faculty senate and union as co-design partners + Harvard RCT-style AI-hybrid teaching model.

fields.label: CONFIDENCE
fields.value: HIGH that universal optional is dominated · MEDIUM-HIGH that the phased-structured path is executable · MEDIUM on regulatory structuring viability

fields.label: DEPENDS ON
fields.value: Required in-person seminars satisfy F-1 SEVP and Title IV RSI · Faculty union accepts co-design · Pilot data credible after 3 years · Mandatory first-year preserves first-gen retention · Harvard RCT replicates at institution scale

fields.label: MONITOR
fields.value: DOE guidance on RSI · SEVP guidance on in-person seminar components · Faculty union calendar · Pilot cohort year-1 outcomes · First-gen retention vs control · Donor sentiment quarterly · ChatGPT-6 capability benchmarks

fields.label: PRE-MORTEM
fields.value: Faculty strikes mid-semester (P=0.15) · Pilot inconclusive from selection bias (P=0.35) · F-1 structured compliance fails legal review (P=0.30) · First-gen dropout despite first-year (P=0.20) · Mid-tier positioning fails (P=0.30)

fields.label: DEEPEST DISAGREEMENT
fields.value: Optimist: 60% Harvard RCT replicates at institution scale [C1:optimist]. Pessimist: 25% [C1:pessimist]. Range 25-60% on whether AI-hybrid pedagogy holds across 30K students. Disagreement IS the data.

fields.label: REVIEW TRIGGER
fields.value: DOE issues negative RSI guidance · SEVP clarifies one-online-course rule strictly · Peer institution announces similar pilot · Pilot year-1 shows negative equity · Faculty union files grievance · ChatGPT-6 obsoletes current AI-hybrid design

slides.source: Source: Decision Brief (full mode, 2 iterations, 5/5 council endorsement on h6) · [G1], [C3:regulator], [C4:regulator], [C13:iter2council]

slides.lead_question: A 5-persona council, 6 competing hypotheses, two iterations to convergence, 12 adversarial stress scenarios — what survived?

slides.title: The analysis explored 6 hypotheses across 2 iterations of a 5-persona council, stress-tested by 12 adversarial scenarios
bullets: 5 personas spawned as separate subagents — Optimist, Pessimist, Competitor, Regulator, Customer — each with independent context
6 hypotheses generated, ranging from universal optional (h1) to phased-structured pilot (h6 — promoted in iteration 2 as the synthesis winner)
2 iterations — convergence reached at iteration 2 with 84% [C1:council] assumption stability and 1 productive contradiction remaining
Adversary phase generated 12 stress scenarios across 4 worst-cases, 4 black swans, and 4 irrational actors
Sensitivity analysis on 8 key assumptions — 4 high-sensitivity, 4 medium
Real-world anchor: Harvard 2025 RCT [G1] showed AI-tutoring effect size 0.73-1.3 SD; the recommendation operationalizes a Harvard-style AI-hybrid teaching model inside a structured pilot.
slides.source: Source: Decision Brief Convergence Log; engine-protocol.md

slides.title: Six hypotheses surfaced; only h6 (phased + structured) survived 5/5 council endorsement and adversarial stress
rows: AI tutoring outperforms · Students self-direct · Faculty adapts
Peer network IS real product · Tuition premium for network
Union contracts strict · Faculty identity load-bearing
Elite resists · Middle squeezed · Online MBA precedent
Revealed preference matches policy · 50-70% [G6] skip routinely
NEW: Phased + structured pilot with safeguards
Pilot bounds risk · Regulatory structuring viable · Faculty partnership

slides.title: Plotted on reversibility × regulatory exposure, h6 sits in the manageable quadrant; h1 sits where every adversarial scenario amplifies

items.label: h1
items.legend: Universal optional — irreversible policy + Title IV/F-1 catastrophe

items.label: h2
items.legend: Social product collapse — risk path of h1, same quadrant

items.label: h3
items.legend: Faculty revolt — triggered by unilateral change

items.label: h5
items.legend: Already optional — true for lectures, not labs/seminars

items.label: h6
items.legend: Phased + structured pilot — preserves optionality, defuses 8/12 adversarial scenarios

slides.source: Source: Synthesis from Decision Brief — bubble size = council_agreement × probability of dominance

slides.lead_question: Where the personas converged, where they diverged, and what the disagreement itself reveals about the decision.

slides.title: The strongest signals point to regulatory catastrophe under universal-optional and structural antifragility under the phased pilot

effects.label: h6 pilot bounds risk to 1-2 departments
Faculty union grievance if unilateral change
Peer network thins as in-person erodes
De facto already optional for lectures (not labs)
AI tutoring outperforms lecture on knowledge transfer
F-1 SEVP violation puts $30-150M international at risk

commentary_lines: Top 12 effects ranked by council_agreement × probability
Bar color = council agreement (navy = 5/5, fading to pale = 1/5)
Black bracket = persona disagreement range — width is the uncertainty signal
Two highest-agreement effects (5/5) are h6 structural protections — the pilot bounds risk and mandatory first-year preserves equity
Regulator's F-1 SEVP finding (1/5, P=0.85) — single-persona but existential; drove h6 dual-track compliance design
slides.source: Source: Decision Brief — Effects Map (top 12 by council_agreement × probability) · [C3:regulator], [C9:council], [C13:iter2council]

slides.title: The deepest disagreement — Harvard RCT replicability — IS the data
left_items: Won on specific regulatory mechanisms — F-1 SEVP one-online-course rule (P=0.85 [C3:regulator]); Title IV RSI reclassification ($50-300M/yr [C4:regulator] aid at risk); FERPA AI-vendor DPA; accreditor substantive-change trigger
Each finding is potentially existential — universal optional is self-enforcing collapse without structured workaround
Surfaced the dual-track compliance filing + required in-person seminar components that became h6's regulatory engine
h6 protected by structuring: regulatory exposure shifts from catastrophic to bounded
right_items: Optimist: 60% [C1:optimist] AI-hybrid replicates at institution scale (Harvard RCT [G1] effect size 0.7-1.3 SD)
Pessimist + Customer: 25% [C1:pessimist] — N=194 lab study won't generalize across 30K students; self-direction heterogeneity bimodal
Range 25-60% on h1 conditional outcome — disagreement IS the data, not noise to average away
Optimist weakest (#5 peer rank) — overweighted Harvard sample, underweighted self-direction variance flagged by Customer
BLIND SPOT caught: equity. Optional attendance is REGRESSIVE inside one tuition tier — high-achievers gain, first-gen lose, no price reduction

slides.title: Regulator scored highest on structural depth and counter-factual rigor; Optimist's narrow ranges drove its #5 peer rank
commentary_lines: Each persona scored 1-5 across five rigor dimensions, derived from peer-review and critique outputs
Regulator's structural depth surfaced both regulatory mechanisms that synthesized into h6 — dual-track compliance and required in-person seminars
Customer caught the equity blind spot — first-gen dropout amplification under universal optional
Optimist's narrow ranges and weak counter-factual depth flagged by 4/4 reviewers — Harvard-RCT generalization optimism
Pessimist + Regulator together cover the full adversarial surface; their alignment on regulatory irreversibility drove h6's safeguards
slides.source: Source: Synthesized from Decision Brief — Council Dynamics + Critique outputs

slides.title: Twelve adversarial scenarios — h6's structured design defuses 8 of them; 4 remain (foundation model, Ivy preempt, campus event, ED admin)

items: Title IV RSI reclassifies as correspondence; $50-300M/yr aid lost (P=0.40)
F-1 SEVP mass violation; 15-25% international revenue drop (P=0.45)
First-gen cohort 30%+ dropout year 1 — equity disaster + Title VI (P=0.35)
Accreditor show-cause letter; focused review; reputational damage (P=0.25)
ChatGPT-6 dramatically better — lecture obsolete, accelerates transition (P=0.30)
Ivy goes optional first; mid-tier first-mover narrative destroyed (P=0.08)
Campus shooting / major event requires in-person community (P=0.10)
ED admin change loosens Title IV; regulatory pressure removed (P=0.35)
Student influencer (5M followers) attacks policy; applications drop (P=0.25)
Faculty union strikes mid-semester; operations chaos (P=0.15)
State legislature passes 'must require in-person' law (P=0.15)

slides.source: Source: Decision Brief — Adversarial Scenarios · [C29:council] through [C36:council]

slides.title: Four high-sensitivity assumptions are load-bearing — if any fails, the universal-optional path becomes indefensible

assumptions.label: F-1 SEVP one-online-course rule enforced against new model
Title IV RSI reclassifies optional+AI as correspondence
Required in-person seminars satisfy SEVP and RSI
Peer network IS the real product of the university
Self-direction is bimodal — top thrives, bottom fails
Union contracts prohibit unilateral attendance change
First-year cohort formation is disproportionately important
AI-hybrid replicates Harvard RCT at institution scale

commentary_lines: Bar length = sensitivity weight × number of effects impacted
Dark-blue bar = HIGH sensitivity, lighter = MEDIUM
Three of four HIGH-sensitivity assumptions are external regulatory interpretations — the university controls none directly
h6's dual-track filing converts 'F-1 SEVP enforced' from catastrophic to bounded: the structure pre-files in-person components that satisfy the rule
If 'required in-person seminars satisfy SEVP+RSI' interpretation fails on legal review (P=0.30), h6's structural workaround fails — pilot must be paused, not the policy reversed
slides.source: Source: Decision Brief — Key Assumptions · sensitivity per effects-impacted × HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW weight

slides.lead_question: Seven gates with kill criteria — every commitment is bounded by faculty, regulator, and pilot data.

slides.title: The recommendation maps to a seven-gate roadmap with explicit kill criteria at every stage
slides.subtitle: Every gate has a binding kill criterion; failure at any gate triggers either pause, redesign, or full rollback
rows: Substantive change filing + dual-track compliance design
SEVP pre-filing + DOE consultation on RSI
Pilot launch in 1-2 departments; mandatory first-year intact
Annual outcome reporting: outcomes, retention, equity
3-year pilot review with external advisory board
Pilot fails → end pilot, maintain current
Phased institution-wide rollout if pilot succeeds
slides.source: Source: Decision Brief — Appendix A: Decision Timeline · phased gates per [C13:iter2council], [C14:iter2council]

Tuition: public $10K/$26K, private $37K, elite $80K total cost

Minerva: 100% online + active learning + rotating cohort; elite alt
AI-hybrid replicates at scale P=0.60 (vs Pessimist 0.25)
Self-direction heterogeneity bimodal P=0.70 — bimodal failure mode
F-1 SEVP one-online-course violation P=0.85; $30-150M international
Title IV RSI reclassification; $50-300M/yr federal aid at risk
H6 pilot bounds risk P=0.75 (5/5 council)
H6 mandatory first-year preserves equity P=0.70 (5/5 council)
